title: Weldment Radiographic Appearance and Acceptance Criteria
narrationScript: This is the lesson where weld radiography earns its money, turning a strip of film into a defensible accept or reject decision. The discipline has an order, and the order is not optional. First prove the film is valid. Density in the code window through the area of interest. The required image quality indicator hole or wire visible. Identification and location markers present. No light image of the letter B from backscatter. Then rule out artifacts. Crimp marks, static, pressure marks, and streaks live in the emulsion, and you can usually catch them by tilting the film in reflected light or finding them on one emulsion side only. Only then do you identify, measure, and apply the code. Now the picture catalog, and remember the rule from castings. Less metal prints dark. Extra metal prints light. Porosity is sharp, dark, rounded spots, scattered, clustered, or strung in a line along a pass. Piping porosity drags a short tail. Slag inclusions are dark but irregular, with angular outlines, usually riding along the bevel sides of the weld image. Wagon tracks print as two broken dark lines paralleling the root image, one on each side of the centerline. Incomplete penetration is one of the easiest calls on film. A straight, dark line dead on the root centerline, with hard straight edges, because the unfused root lands are machined or ground surfaces. It can be continuous or intermittent. Lack of fusion is its sly cousin. Also a straightish dark line, but offset from the centerline, following the fusion face along the bevel, often slightly angled to the weld image edge, and usually tighter and fainter, because the beam rarely lines up perfectly with the fusion plane. Cracks print as fine, dark, wandering lines, often with tails that fade to nothing at the tips. A crack can look like a hair on the film. If beam alignment is unfavorable, it may barely print at all, which is why a suspected crack justifies extra angles. Undercut shows as a dark, slightly fuzzy band hugging the toe of the weld image, just outside the weld metal, and you can often confirm it visually on the part. Root concavity is a broad, smooth darkening along the root centerline, where the root surface dished inward. Tell it from incomplete penetration by its soft, diffuse edges. The light images. Excess reinforcement and excess penetration print light, more metal in the beam, with excess penetration making light lobes along the root, the icicles. Burn through is the violent version, a localized dark blowout, often with a light rim of sagged metal around it. Tungsten inclusions are unmistakable, bright light specks, because tungsten is far denser than steel. Misalignment, high low, shows as an abrupt density step along the weld where one wall stands proud of the other. Now acceptance. Welds are not graded against reference plates the way castings are. Weld codes use workmanship criteria, written limits in the contract document, and the structure repeats across the industry. First, the zero tolerance class. Cracks, lack of fusion, and incomplete penetration are rejected outright by most fabrication codes, regardless of length. No measuring debate. Second, the measured class. Slag and elongated indications are limited by length tied to wall thickness, commonly a fraction of the wall for a single inclusion and a total allowance within a running window of weld length. Undercut is limited by depth, often a fraction of the wall with an absolute cap. Reinforcement gets a height limit by thickness. Third, the compared class. Rounded indications, porosity, are judged against the code's porosity charts, pictogram style pages showing the maximum size and concentration permitted for the wall thickness. You compare the film to the chart much like a casting interpreter uses reference plates. One convention to burn in. Codes commonly treat an indication as rounded if its length is no more than three times its width, and as linear if longer than that, and the two classes carry different limits. And one boundary to respect. The criteria come from the governing code and contract. Structural work, pipelines, and pressure equipment each carry their own tables, and the same slag line can pass one code and fail another. Your authority as a Level Two is to identify honestly, measure honestly, and apply the specified table exactly. The film is the evidence. The code is the law. Keep those straight and your calls survive any audit.
imagePrompt: Two radiographers in a darkened film-reading room lean over a high-intensity illuminator examining a freshly processed pipe weld radiograph, one holding a comparator scale against a fine dark line near the weld image centerline.

sections.heading: Discipline Before Diagnosis
sections.body:
An evaluation is only as good as the film it rides on, so the reading order is fixed:

- **Prove the film valid** — density inside the code window through the area of interest, required IQI hole or wire visible, identification and location markers present, no light **B** image from backscatter.
- **Rule out artifacts** — crimp (crescent) marks, static, pressure marks, and processing streaks live **in the emulsion**: tilt the film in reflected light, or check whether the image sits on one emulsion side only.
- **Then identify, measure, and apply the code** — in that order.

Calling a crescent-shaped crimp mark a crack rejects a sound weld; missing a real crack because the density was too thin to see it certifies a defective one. The validity checks are not bureaucracy — they are what makes the interpretation mean something.

sections.caption: Simulated weld radiograph with the classic image of each major weld discontinuity labeled.

sections.heading: The Image Catalog
sections.body:
Less metal prints **dark**; extra metal prints **light**.

- **Porosity** — sharp dark rounded spots: scattered, clustered, or aligned along a pass. **Piping porosity** drags a short tail.
- **Slag** — dark, irregular, angular outlines, riding the bevel sides of the weld image; **wagon tracks** are two broken dark lines paralleling the root centerline.
- **Incomplete penetration** — straight dark line **on the root centerline** with hard, straight edges (the unfused lands are prepared surfaces); continuous or intermittent.
- **Lack of fusion** — straightish dark line **offset from centerline**, following the bevel; usually tighter and fainter because the beam rarely aligns with the fusion plane.
- **Cracks** — fine, dark, **wandering** lines with tails fading to nothing; may barely print if beam alignment is unfavorable.
- **Undercut** — dark, slightly fuzzy band hugging the **toe**, just outside the weld metal; confirm on the part surface.
- **Root concavity** — broad smooth darkening along the root with **diffuse** edges (contrast with IP's hard edges).
- **Excess reinforcement / excess penetration** — **light** areas; icicles print as light lobes along the root. **Burn-through** — localized dark blowout, often ringed by lighter sagged metal.
- **Tungsten inclusions** — bright **light** specks (tungsten is far denser than steel). **Hi-lo** — an abrupt density step along one side of the root image.

sections.heading: Look-Alikes and How to Split Them
sections.body:
Four dark lines cause most of the bad calls:

- **IP vs. LOF** — IP sits dead on the root centerline with hard straight edges; LOF runs offset, along the bevel direction, often slightly angled to the weld image edge.
- **IP vs. root concavity** — both darken the root zone, but concavity is broad and soft-edged (a dished surface), while IP is narrow and hard-edged (unfused lands). When available, a look at the actual root or a comparator density check settles it.
- **Crack vs. slag line** — slag has width, body, and angular irregularity; a crack is finer, wanders, and fades at its tips.
- **Crack vs. scratch or static** — artifacts live in the emulsion: tilt the film under reflected light and check both emulsion surfaces before condemning the weld.

When the image stays ambiguous, the professional answers are more information — another exposure at a changed angle — or referral, never a guess recorded as fact.

Measure with the right tools: a transparent **comparator scale** laid on the film for lengths, and the **densitometer** when a density difference needs to be quantified rather than eyeballed. Record indication lengths as measured — acceptance math is done against the recorded number, not a recollection.

sections.caption: Weld discontinuity images and their typical code treatment.
rows: Fine, dark, wandering line with fading tails
Tight dark line offset along the bevel
Straight hard-edged dark line on root centerline
Length limits tied to wall thickness over a running window
Compared to code porosity charts by thickness
Fuzzy dark band at the toe
Depth limit, often with absolute cap
Treated under inclusion limits; light image

sections.heading: Acceptance Criteria in the Real Codes
sections.body:
Welds are not graded against reference plates the way castings are. Weld codes use **workmanship criteria** — written limits in the governing document — and the structure repeats across the industry:

- **Zero-tolerance class**: cracks, lack of fusion, and incomplete penetration are rejected outright by most fabrication codes, regardless of length.
- **Measured class**: slag and other elongated indications carry **length limits tied to wall thickness** — commonly a fraction of the wall for a single indication plus a cumulative allowance within a running window of weld length. Undercut is limited by **depth**; reinforcement by **height**.
- **Compared class**: rounded indications are judged against the code's **porosity charts** — pictogram-style pages showing maximum size and concentration for the wall thickness, used much like casting reference plates.

A convention to burn in: codes commonly treat an indication as **rounded** when its length is no more than **three times its width**, and **linear** beyond that — and the two classes carry different limits. Codes also restrict **aligned** rounded indications more tightly than scattered ones, because a row of pores acts like a single long flaw. Structural codes, pipeline codes, and pressure-equipment codes each publish their own tables; the same slag line can pass one and fail another. The governing code is named in the contract — apply that one, exactly.

sections.caption: The six-step evaluation sequence from film validity to documented disposition.

sections.title: The film is the evidence; the code is the law
sections.body: A Level II's authority is to **identify honestly, measure honestly, and apply the specified table exactly** — never to decide what the weld can tolerate. If asked to round down a measurement or accept past the limit, the answer is the documented reading, the code clause, and escalation to the Level III and the customer.

interactive.title: Find the Incomplete Penetration
interactive.intro: Film quality has been verified: density 2.6, required wire visible, markers present. Six indications are labeled on this weld image. Click the incomplete penetration.

regions.label: Rounded dark spots
regions.feedback: Sharp, dark, rounded spots are porosity — trapped gas. Rounded indications get compared to the code's porosity charts, not rejected as IP.

regions.label: Irregular dark trail
regions.feedback: Dark but irregular and angular, riding the bevel side of the weld image — slag. Measured against length limits, not the IP call.

regions.label: Straight line on centerline
regions.feedback: Correct. A straight dark line dead on the root centerline with hard, straight edges — the unfused root lands. Incomplete penetration, rejected by most fabrication codes.

regions.label: Straight line off centerline
regions.feedback: Straightish and dark, but offset from the centerline and angled along the bevel — lack of fusion on the fusion face, not IP. Also rejectable, but the wrong identification fails audits.

regions.label: Fine wandering line
regions.feedback: Fine, wandering, fading at the tips — a crack. The most serious call on the film, but not IP: IP is straight and hard-edged on the centerline.

regions.label: Bright light speck
regions.feedback: A light image means more absorption, not less — a tungsten inclusion from a dipped TIG electrode. IP always prints dark.

checkQuestions.question: A bright, light speck appears in the root area of a TIG-welded stainless joint. This indicates:
options: A gas pore that collapsed during cooling
A tungsten inclusion — tungsten is far denser than steel, so it absorbs more radiation and prints light
Burn-through at the root
A developer splash artifact that must print light
checkQuestions.explanation: Light images mean more metal or denser metal in the beam path; a chip of dipped tungsten electrode is the classic bright speck in GTAW roots.

checkQuestions.question: Which image character points to incomplete penetration rather than lack of fusion?
options: A straight dark line precisely on the root centerline with hard, straight edges
A tight dark line offset from centerline, angled along the bevel
A broad, soft-edged darkening across the root area
A fine wandering line with fading tails
checkQuestions.explanation: IP leaves the prepared root lands unfused, printing a hard-edged straight dark line on the centerline; LOF follows the bevel offset from center, concavity is soft-edged, and the wanderer is a crack.

checkQuestions.question: An indication on a weld film measures 0.12 in. long and 0.03 in. wide. Under the common code convention it is classified as:
options: Rounded, because its length is no more than three times its width
Linear, because its length exceeds three times its width
Rounded, because it is under a quarter inch long
Unclassifiable without a densitometer reading
checkQuestions.explanation: 0.12 divided by 0.03 is 4 — longer than three times the width — so it is treated as a linear indication and judged under length limits, not the porosity charts.

checkQuestions.question: Which group of discontinuities do most fabrication codes reject outright, with no length measurement debate?
options: Porosity, undercut, and reinforcement
Cracks, lack of fusion, and incomplete penetration
Slag, hi-lo, and root concavity
Tungsten inclusions, spatter, and arc strikes
checkQuestions.explanation: Cracks, LOF, and IP are planar, crack-like defects with severe service consequences, so the workmanship tables of most fabrication codes reject any amount.

checkQuestions.question: Root concavity and incomplete penetration both darken the root image. The reliable film distinction is:
options: Concavity prints light, IP prints dark
Concavity is a broad darkening with smooth, diffuse edges; IP is a narrow line with hard, straight edges
Concavity appears only at weld stops; IP only at starts
There is no distinction — both are reported as IP
checkQuestions.explanation: Suck-back is a dished surface, so its density change is gradual and soft-edged; IP is a gap between machined lands, so its edges are sharp and straight — and the two carry different code dispositions.

references: CP-105-2024 RT Topical Outline, Radiographic Interpretation and Evaluation Course, IE 4.4–4.6
Workmanship acceptance tables in structural, pipeline, and pressure-equipment codes (general reference)
Code porosity comparison charts for rounded indications (general reference)
